1: peri men gar tēs diakonias tēs eis tous hagious: 'Now it is superfluous for me to write to you about the ministry for the saints.' The chapter resumes the collection theme with a different rhetorical strategy: Paul claims the Corinthians already know enough that writing is superfluous, then proceeds to write at length. This is the praeteritio (passing-over) rhetorical device: ostensibly declining to discuss a subject while doing exactly that. Diakonias (ministry/service) applied to the financial collection elevates giving to the status of the same word used for apostolic ministry (3:7-9; 4:1) — economic generosity is itself a form of gospel ministry.
2: oida gar tēn prothumian hymōn hēn hyper hymōn kauchōmai Makedosin hoti Achaia pareskeuastai apo perusi: 'For I know your readiness, of which I boast about you to the people of Macedonia, saying that Achaia has been ready since last year.' Prothumia (eagerness/readiness) — the disposition-word Paul uses throughout chs. 8-9 for genuine voluntary generosity. The boasting reversal: Paul has been holding up the Macedonians to the Corinthians (8:1-5), and now reveals he has been holding up the Corinthians to the Macedonians. Each community motivates the other — apostolic diplomacy using comparative praise. Pareskeuastai (has been prepared, perfect passive) — the state of readiness is already complete in Paul's representation of them.
3: epempsa de tous adelphous hina mē to kauchēma hēmōn to hyper hymōn kenōthē en tō merei toutō: 'But I am sending the brothers so that our boasting about you may not prove empty in this matter.' Kenos/kenoō (empty/emptied/nullified) — the recurrent 'vain/empty' word: Paul does not want his boast about the Corinthians to be 'emptied out' (kenōthē, aorist passive subjunctive) by their failure to complete the collection. Hina paraskeuasmenoi ēte: 'in order that you may be ready' — Titus and the brothers will prepare them so that Paul's boast about their readiness is true when Paul himself arrives.
4: mēpōs ean elthōsin syn emoi Makedones kai eurōsin hymas aparaskeuous kataischynthōmen hēmeis: 'Otherwise, if some Macedonians come with me and find that you are not ready, we — to say nothing of you — would be humiliated.' Kataischynō (put to shame, humiliate) — the conditional scenario deployed to motivate: the honor of both Paul and the Corinthians is at stake. Aparaskeuous (unprepared) — the opposite of pareskeuasmenoi (ready) in v. 3. The conditional (ean ... eurōsin, if they should find you unprepared) uses a third-class conditional (real possibility) rather than a remote hypothetical. The social stakes are concretely articulated.
5: anagkaion oun hēgēsamēn parakalesai tous adelphous hina proelthōsin eis hymas kai prokatartisōsin tēn prokategorian eulogian hymōn tautēn: 'So I thought it necessary to urge the brothers to go on ahead to you and arrange in advance for the gift you have promised, so that it may be ready as a willing gift, not as an exaction.' Prokatartizō (arrange in advance, make ready beforehand) — hapax in NT; the pro- prefix emphasizes advance preparation. Eulogia (blessing/gift/bounty) — Paul uses eulogia for the collection, elevating it to a 'blessed gift.' The contrast: eulogia (blessing/generous gift) vs. pleonexia (covetousness/extortion) — the same gift can be either depending on the motivation and manner of its extraction.
6: touto de ho speirōn pheidomenōs pheidomenōs kai therisei kai ho speirōn ep eulogiais ep eulogiais kai therisei: 'The point is this: whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully.' The agricultural sowing-reaping principle as the theological logic of generosity. Pheidomenōs (sparingly, frugally) — an adverb from pheidomai (to spare, be frugal). Ep' eulogiais (upon blessings/bountifully) — the phrase is used twice, framing generous giving as a blessing-field. The principle is eschatological: the harvest corresponds to the sowing, but the reaping is God's future act (cf. Gal 6:7-8 for the same sowing/reaping logic in the moral register).
7: hekastos kathōs proēretai tē kardia mē ek lypēs ē ex anagkēs hilaron gar dotēn agapa ho theos: 'Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.' The decisive phrase: proēretai (has decided beforehand, from proaireō — to choose before, prefer) — the decision precedes the giving; the heart's prior commitment makes the act genuine. Ek lypēs (out of grief/reluctance) and ex anagkēs (out of compulsion/necessity) are the two invalid motivations. Hilaron dotēn (cheerful/joyful giver): a near-citation of LXX Prov 22:8a in some versions; hilaros (cheerful, from which 'hilarious') — joyful inner freedom is the mark of genuine generosity.
8: dynatos de ho theos pasan charin perisseuein eis hymas hina en panti pantote pasan autarkeian echontes perisseuēte eis pan ergon agathon: 'And God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work.' Autarkeia (sufficiency/contentment/self-sufficiency) — the key Stoic philosophical term for the sage's inner independence from external circumstances. Paul 'baptizes' the Stoic term: autarkeia is not self-achieved independence but God-provided sufficiency that enables generosity. The six-fold panta/pan (all/every) repetition: all grace, all sufficiency, all things, all times, all, every good work — the comprehensive divine provision for comprehensive generous living.
9: kathōs gegraptai eskorpisen edōken tois penēsin hē dikaiosynē autou menei eis ton aiōna: Citation of LXX Ps 111:9 (Ps 112:9 ET). Eskorpisen (scattered/distributed) — the image of scattering implies generous, widespread distribution. Tois penēsin (to the poor) — penēs (the working poor who lack resources) rather than ptōchos (the destitute beggar). Hē dikaiosynē autou menei eis ton aiōna (his righteousness abides forever) — Paul applies the psalm's description of the righteous generous person to the character of God who ultimately enables all generosity; the dikaiosynē (righteousness) that endures is both human (the giver's moral act) and divine (God's own character).
10: ho de epichorēgōn sporan tō speirōn kai arton eis brōsin chorēgēsei kai plēthynei tēn sporar hymōn kai auxēsei ta genēmata tēs dikaiosynēs hymōn: 'He who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply your seed for sowing and increase the harvest of your righteousness.' Epichorēgōn and chorēgēsei — both from chorēgeō (provide/supply, from chorēgos, the wealthy Athenian citizen who funded dramatic productions). Plēthynō (multiply) and auxanō (increase, grow) are both divine agricultural verbs: God multiplies the capital (seed) and increases the yield (righteousness-harvest). The citation resonates with Isa 55:10 (rain and snow making seed bread): divine provision cycles through creation for human flourishing.
11: en panti ploutizomenoi eis pasan haplotēta hētis katergazetai di hēmōn eucharistian tō theō: 'You will be enriched in every way to be generous in every way, which through us will produce thanksgiving to God.' Haplotēs (generosity/simplicity) — the governing virtue of the collection discourse: singlehearted generosity that produces thanksgiving. The causal chain: Corinthian enrichment → their generosity → Paul's mediation → Jerusalem's thanksgiving to God. The collection's ultimate purpose is doxological: eucharistia (thanksgiving) to God, not merely economic relief. The Corinthians' gift will generate waves of prayer and praise across the network.
12: hoti hē diakonia tēs leitourgias tautēs ou monon estin prosanaplērousa ta hysterēmata tōn hagiōn alla kai perisseuousa dia pollōn eucharistiōn tō theō: 'for the ministry of this service is not only supplying the needs of the saints but is also overflowing in many thanksgivings to God.' Leitourgia (public service, liturgical ministry) — the Greek civic word for a wealthy citizen's obligation to fund a public service at personal expense; in LXX it refers to Levitical cultic service; Paul uses it for the collection, elevating it to a quasi-liturgical act. Prosanaplērousa ta hysterēmata (supplying the deficiencies/needs) — the economic function. Perisseuousa dia pollōn eucharistiōn (overflowing through many thanksgivings) — the doxological surplus beyond the economic transaction.
13: dia tēs dokimēs tēs diakonias tautēs doxazontes ton theon epi tē hypotagē tēs homologias hymōn eis to euangelion tou Christou: 'By their approval of this service, they will glorify God because of your submission that comes from your confession of the gospel of Christ.' Dokimē (proven character, test-result) — the collection proves the Corinthians' character to Jerusalem. Hypotagē tēs homologias (submission of the confession, or confession's obedience) — the genitive is ambiguous: either 'the obedience that belongs to your confession' or 'the submission demonstrated by your confession.' The giving enacts the gospel confession — economic solidarity is a form of confessional speech. Koinōnias eis autous kai eis pantas: the liberal sharing extends to all.
14: kai autōn deēsei hyper hymōn epipothountōn hymas dia tēn hyperballen charin tou theou eph hymin: 'while they long for you and pray for you, because of the surpassing grace of God upon you.' Epipothountes (yearning for, longing for) — the deep emotional attachment created by the economic gift; the collection creates a bond of prayer and longing between Jerusalem and Corinth. Hyperballousa charis tou theou (surpassing/exceeding grace of God) — the hyper- prefix expresses excess and overflow, characteristic of Paul's grace-vocabulary. Jerusalem will recognize the Corinthians' gift as evidence of God's surpassing grace at work in the Gentile churches — reconciling the Jewish-Gentile divide through economic solidarity.
15: charis tō theō epi tē anekdiēgētō autou dōrea: 'Thanks be to God for his inexpressible gift.' Anekdiēgētos (inexpressible, indescribable) — a NT hapax legomenon; the compound an- (not) + ekdiēgeomai (describe fully, narrate completely). The word is a superlative of divine grace: whatever the collection accomplishes, God's own gift exceeds description. The chapter closes doxologically (as does 8:16 with charis de tō theō): the entire argument of chs. 8-9 begins and ends with God's grace as the source and goal of all human giving. The inexpressible gift is most naturally Christ himself, though Paul leaves the identification to the reader's recognition.
